results.possibleIdentity: Joel Schesser is an electrical engineer and educator who was active as a student and professional in the New York area during the late 1960s and 1970s. He earned his Bachelor of Engineering (B.E.) in 1968, followed by a Master of Engineering (M.E.) in 1971, and a Ph.D. in 1976, all from the City University of New York (CUNY), with his studies centered at the City College of New York (CCNY). During the late 1960s, he was an electrical engineering student at CCNY, which aligns with his identification as a student and electrical engineer based in the Bronx/New York area. Schesser had a significant professional career at AT&T Bell Laboratories, where he contributed to the development of undersea fiber optic cable systems, including the architecture for the Africa Optical Network (Africa ONE), and worked on the design requirements for optical submarine cable systems. He was also involved in the design and implementation of traffic surveillance and control systems, such as the Hampton Roads Bridge-Tunnel Crossing system in 1977. Later in his career, he became a Senior University Lecturer in the Department of Biomedical Engineering at the New Jersey Institute of Technology (NJIT). His early education includes graduating from the High School of Music & Art (M&A) in New York in 1963, a background that supports a potential association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization known for fostering collaborations between artists and engineers from institutions like Bell Labs and CCNY.
